coredata.dc:description: Infections caused by multidrug-resistant (MDR) bacteria have become a major challenge for healthcare systems worldwide. Antibacterial carbon dots (CDs) have received increasing attention for fighting MDR bacteria. In this study, we synthesized nitrogen and sulfur self-doped garlic carbon dots (GCDs) via a one-step hydrothermal method. The prepared GCDs are sphere-like nanoparticles with graphite lattice structure and positive charge (+8.53 mV). The minimum inhibitory concentrations (MICs) of GCDs against methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA), multidrug-resistant Salmonella Typhimurium (MRST), and generic Escherichia coli were 25 μg/mL, 75 μg/mL, and 50 μg/mL, respectively. The MICs of kanamycin against MRSA and MRST were approximately 64 and 18 times higher than those of GCDs, respectively. GCDs at 200 μg/mL eliminated all three bacteria within 180 min. GCDs also effectively inhibited the biofilm formation of MRSA and MRST in a dose-dependent manner. The antimicrobial mechanisms of GCDs against MDR bacteria include: (1) electrostatic interaction facilitated penetration into MDR bacteria; (2) disruption of cell structure and subsequent leakage of intracellular components; (3) induction of oxidative stress and inhibition of the antioxidant enzymes. In addition, GCDs showed high biocompatibility in hemolysis assays and vitro cytotoxicity assays. In conclusion, this study developed a novel antimicrobial nanomaterial based on natural plants, providing an important idea for the rational use of natural biomass against MDR bacterial infections.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of the GCDs (inset: HRTEM lattice fringe images). (B) Particle size distributions of GCDs. (C) Excitation (Ex), emission (Em) spectra and UV–Vis absorption of GCDs. The insets show the photographs of the GCDs suspensions under fluorescent lamps (a) and under 365 nm UV radiation (b). (D) Fluorescence (FL) emission spectra under different excitation wavelengths. (E) FTIR spectrum of GCDs. (F) XPS survey spectrum of GCDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of (G)C1s, (H)O1s, (I)N1s.
MICs of kanamycin, rifampicin, garlic juice and GCDs against E. coli, MRSA, and MRST. (n = 5, mean ± SD, for each strain, different letters mean significant differences, p < 0.05).
Bactericidal effect of garlic juice and GCDs on (A) E. coli, (B) MRSA, and (C) MRST. Time-kill curves of GCDs for (D) E. coli, (E) MRSA, and (F) MRST. (n = 3, mean ± SD.)

(A) Crystal violet staining image of MRSA and MRST biofilms treated with different concentrations of GCDs under optical microscope. Inhibition percentage of (B) MRSA and (C) MRST biofilms formation with various concentrations of GCDs. (n = 4, mean ± SD). Within GCDs concentration, means with different letters are significantly different (p < 0.05).

CLSM images of (A) MRSA and (B) MRST with the treatment of 200 μg/mL GCDs (treated for 60 min and 120 min) with SYTO 9 (labels live bacteria) and PI (labels dead bacteria) (scale bar = 20 μm). SEM images of (C) MRSA and (D) MRST treated with 200 μg/mL GCDs for 60 min and 120 min.

(A, B) AKP activity, (C, D) the protein concentration and (E, F) the content of nucleic acids of MRSA and MRST with the treatment of GCDs (50, 100, and 200 μg/mL) at time interval 0, 60, 120 and 180min, respectively. (n = 3, mean ± SD, different letters a–f mean significant differences, p < 0.05.)

(A) Zeta potentials of GCDs, MRSA, and MRST. (B) CLSM images of MRSA and MRST treated with 50 μg/mL GCDs for 45 min (excitation wavelength = 405 nm, emission was collected at 415–550 nm, scale bar = 10 μm). (n = 5, mean ± SD, different letters mean significant differences, p < 0.05).

Fluorescence spectra of adding DCFH-DA into (A) MRSA and (B) MRST solution after treatment with GCDs (50, 100, and 200 μg/mL) for 30 min. Rosup is a positive control reagent for ROS. (C, D) SOD and (E, F) CAT activity of MRSA and MRST with the treatment of GCDs (50, 100, and 200 μg/mL) at time interval 0, 15, 30, and 60min, respectively. (n = 3, mean ± SD, different letters a–d mean significant differences at the same GCDs concentration, p < 0.05.)

Relative viabilities of (A) HEpG2, (B) PC-12 and (C) EA. hy926 cells exposed to different concentrations of GCDs for 24 h.(D) Hemolysis results of different concentrations of CDs. The insets show photographs of corresponding RBC solutions. No significant differences in cell viability were observed for each cell line regardless of the CDs concentrations (n = 3, mean ± SD.)
Comparisons of the MICs of GCDs and other CDs from different carbon sources for E. coli., S. aureus, MRSA, and MRST.

head.ce:title: Natural biomass-derived carbon dots as potent antimicrobial agents against multidrug-resistant bacteria and their biofilms

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Infections caused by multidrug-resistant (MDR) bacteria have become a major challenge for healthcare systems worldwide. Antibacterial carbon dots (CDs) have received increasing attention for fighting MDR bacteria. In this study, we synthesized nitrogen and sulfur self-doped garlic carbon dots (GCDs) via a one-step hydrothermal method. The prepared GCDs are sphere-like nanoparticles with graphite lattice structure and positive charge (+8.53 mV). The minimum inhibitory concentrations (MICs) of GCDs against methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA), multidrug-resistant Salmonella Typhimurium (MRST), and generic Escherichia coli were 25 μg/mL, 75 μg/mL, and 50 μg/mL, respectively. The MICs of kanamycin against MRSA and MRST were approximately 64 and 18 times higher than those of GCDs, respectively. GCDs at 200 μg/mL eliminated all three bacteria within 180 min. GCDs also effectively inhibited the biofilm formation of MRSA and MRST in a dose-dependent manner. The antimicrobial mechanisms of GCDs against MDR bacteria include: (1) electrostatic interaction facilitated penetration into MDR bacteria; (2) disruption of cell structure and subsequent leakage of intracellular components; (3) induction of oxidative stress and inhibition of the antioxidant enzymes. In addition, GCDs showed high biocompatibility in hemolysis assays and vitro cytotoxicity assays. In conclusion, this study developed a novel antimicrobial nanomaterial based on natural plants, providing an important idea for the rational use of natural biomass against MDR bacterial infections.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Bacterial infections are an important challenge for healthcare systems [1]. The emergence of multidrug-resistant (MDR) bacteria has exacerbated this challenge and become a global crisis [2]. MDR bacteria cause nearly 3 million infections and 48,000 deaths in the United States each year [3], and the cost of treatment is estimated to be $50,000 per person [4]. The formation of biofilm, which is responsible for >80% of clinical infections [5], further compromises the effect of antibiotic treatments. Therefore, effective alternative antimicrobials are urgently needed. The recent boom in nanotechnology has given rise to many promising antimicrobial nanomaterials such as nanopolymers [6–8], metal/metal oxide nanoparticles [9,10], semiconductor nanoparticles [11], nanoemulsions [12,13], liposomes [14–16], and carbon-based nanomaterials [17,18]. Some nanomaterials can also be combined with various functional materials and theranostic agents to produce intrinsically therapeutic nanoparticles, which have antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, antiangiogenic, and antibacterial properties [19,20]. Notably, unlike conventional antibiotics with specific targets, nanomaterials have multiple targets and apply various antimicrobial mechanisms, thus resistance is less likely to emerge [21–24].

Carbon dots (CDs), a class of 0-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, have attracted increasing interest due to their unique optical properties, ease of modification and functionalization, extremely small size, excellent water dispersibility, good biocompatibility and ease of synthesis [25,26]. Based on these properties, CDs are widely used in bioimaging [27], photocatalysis [28], optical devices [29], biosensors [30], photodynamic therapy [31], and targeted drug delivery [32]. The aforementioned CDs are typically synthesized with organic solvents, inorganic salts, amines, and organic acids. Yang et al. used a solvothermal method to synthesize quaternary CDs that can be used against Staphylococcus aureus, Micrococcus luteus, and Bacillus subtilis infections [33]. Folic acid CDs developed by Yu et al. inhibited biofilm formation of S. aureus [34]. In a previous study, we developed a hydrogel using spermidine CDs, which effectively prevented infections in rabbits caused by MDR bacteria [17]. Despite the promising antimicrobial efficacy of CDs synthesized using chemical reagents, safety concerns of raw materials and by-products as well as high costs have hindered their application [35,36]. Therefore, there is a need to explore more inexpensive and eco-friendly green raw materials to synthesize CDs.

Biomass is rich in organic acids and biopolyamines, which facilitate the self-doping of carbon dots heteroatoms, making it an environmentally friendly, biocompatible, and cost-effective raw material for the synthesis of CDs [37]. For example, Panda et al. developed a NIR-excited fluorescent antibacterial gel based on papaya leaf CDs with antimicrobial effect against S. aureus and Vibrio chlerae [38]. Wang et al. prepared antibacterial CDs using Artemisia argyi leaves, which were able to selectively kill Gram-negative bacteria [39]. Despite the excellent biocompatibility, antimicrobial efficacy of these biomass-derived CDs is typically inferior than that of chemical-derived CDs [36]. More efforts are needed to explore biomass-derived CDs with a higher antimicrobial efficacy.

It is worth noting that the synthesis process usually retains some of the properties of the precursor material [40]. Thus, CDs synthesized from natural antimicrobial plants may have excellent biocompatibility while retaining the antimicrobial ability of the plants themselves. In this study, we synthesized nitrogen and sulfur self-doped antimicrobial CDs using a simple one-step hydrothermal method with garlic, a natural antibacterial plant [41], as the carbon source free of any organic reagents. The characterization results showed that garlic CDs (GCDs) had a positive surface charge, excellent fluorescence properties, and very small particle size, which are typical of carbon dots. The MIC test results demonstrated that GCDs were far more effective than traditional antibiotics and comparable to chemical-derived CDs in fighting MDR bacteria. And GCDs were able to inhibit the biofilm formation of MDR bacteria. In addition, GCDs, as biomass-derived CDs, have proven to be highly biocompatible in cytotoxicity assays and hemolysis tests. Meanwhile, in order to elucidate the antibacterial mechanism of GCDs, we investigated the growth, morphological changes, cell wall and cell membrane integrity, reactive oxygen species (ROS) levels, and antioxidant enzyme activities of bacterial cells after GCDs treatments.

Garlic was purchased from Wuxi Agricultural Products Market in Jiangsu Province. Phosphate buffer solution (PBS), CCK-8 kit, alkaline phosphatase (AKP), BCA protein, superoxide dismutase (SOD), catalase (CAT), and ROS assay kit were purchased from Biyuntian Biotechnology Co., (Shanghai, China). Kanamycin, rifampin, Luria-Bertani (LB), Trypticase Soy Broth (TSB), and Nutrient Agar (NA) medium were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., (Shanghai, China). SYTO 9/PI Live/Dead Bacterial Double Stain Kit purchased from Maokang Biotechnology Co., (Shanghai, China). Human hepatocellular carcinoma cells (HEpG2), rat adrenal pheochromocytoma cells (PC-12), and human umbilical vein cell fusion cells (EA. hy926) were obtained from the Cell Bank of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (Shanghai, China). All other chemicals were analytical pure reagents.

GCDs was synthesized by a simple hydrothermal synthesis method. Briefly, 20 g of garlic cloves and 80 mL of ultra-pure water were added into the juice extractor and blended for 2 min. The resulting mixture was filtrated through a double-loop qualitative filter paper to obtain the garlic juice. Then 40 mL of the garlic juice was transferred into 50 mL poly (tetrafluoroethylene)-lined autoclaves to synthesize GCDs. After reaction at 210 °C for 4 h, the products were cooled naturally to room temperature. After centrifugation at 14, 000rpm for 20min, the GCDs suspension was passed through a 0.22 μm filter. The resulting CDs solution was dialyzed in a 500 Da dialysis bag with ultra-pure water for 6 h, and the dialysate was changed hourly. The prepared CDs were vacuum freeze-dried and stored at 4 °C before use.

High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images of GCDs were obtained on a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (JEOL Ltd., Japan) at 200 kV. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra of GCDs were recorded with a Lambda EZ201 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer, USA). Fluorescence spectra (excitation and emission) were collected using a F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was performed with a D2 PHASER diffractometer (Bruker, Germany) from 5° to 70°. The Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) image was recorded on a Nicolet iS50 FT-IR (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) from 650 to 4000 cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) of GCDs were detected using a K-Alpha+ X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) with Al Kα excitation. The zeta potentials (ζ) of GCDs and bacteria were recorded with a Nano ZS Zetasizer (Malvern Instruments, UK).

Escherichia coli, methicillin-resistant S. aureus (MRSA), and multidrug-resistant Salmonella Typhimurium (MRST) were stored in our lab [17]. All three bacterial strains were incubated in Luria-Bertani (LB) medium at 37 °C overnight under shaking at 220 rpm. When the optical density at 600 nm (OD600) reached 1.0, the suspension was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 10 min at 4 °C, and the pellets were washed 3 times and resuspended in phosphate buffer before use.

Samples (GCDs and garlic juice) were 2-fold serially diluted in 96-well plates using LB. Each inoculum was diluted with LB and added to 96-well plates to a final concentration of 1 × 104 to 1 × 105 CFU/mL. The 96-well plate was incubated at 37 °C for 24 h, and the turbidity of each well was checked visually. MIC is determined as the lowest concentration at which no bacterial growth was observed.

The bactericidal effect was evaluated by mixing each strain (1 × 106 to 1 × 107 CFU/mL) with various concentrations of garlic juice or GCDs (0, 25, 50, 100, and 200 μg/mL) in PBS. After shaking at 240 rpm for 240 min at 37 °C, 100 μL of each bacterial suspension was spread on LB agar plates and incubated at 37 °C for 18 h. GCDs treated bacterial suspensions were also sampled at 0, 15, 30, 60, 90, 120, 180, and 240 min to generate the time-kill curves.

MRSA and MRST were propagated in TSB, mixed with GCDs solutions, and added to 96-well plates. The final bacterial concentration in each well was 1 × 107 CFU/mL and the concentrations of GCDs were 0, 50, 100, 200, 400, 800, and 1600 μg/mL. The 96-well plates were incubated at 37 °C for 36 h and biofilm was quantified using crystal violet assay [42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: CLSM and SEM characterization of the bacteria
ce:section.ce:para: Each strain was treated with 200 μg/mL of GCDs for 0, 60, and 120 min, and centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 8 min. One set of bacterial pellets were resuspended in sterile PBS, stained with a mixture of SYTO 9 and propidium iodide (PI) dyes for 15 min at room temperature, and observed using a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM, Olympus PV3000, Japan). Another set of bacterial pellets were fixed with 2.5% glutaraldehyde for 12 h, dehydrated using graded alcohol, freeze-dried, and observed using a scanning electron microscope (Hitachi SU8010, Japan). Those untreated with GCDs served as the control group.

Each strain was treated with different concentrations of GCDs (0, 50, 100, and 200 μg/mL) for 0, 60,120, and 180 min. The cell suspensions were centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 8 min. The supernatant was added to a 96-well plate, and the OD260 values were measured using a microplate reader (MQX200, BioTek, USA). The protein concentration and AKP activity in the supernatant were then measured using BCA protein assay kit and AKP assay kit, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the interactions between GCDs and bacterial cells
ce:section.ce:para: Bacterial suspensions were mixed with GCDs solution. The mixture was shaken at 220 rpm for 45 min at 37 °C, and then centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 5 min. The bacterial cells treated with GCDs were observed, using CLSM. The zeta potentials (ζ) of GCDs and bacterial suspensions were recorded on a Zetasizer (Nano ZS, Malvern, UK).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of intracellular ROS and antioxidant enzyme activity
ce:section.ce:para: Concentrations of ROS, SOD, and CAT activities in bacteria treated with different concentrations of GCDs were tested following previous publication [43]. Bacteria treated with the ROS initiator (Rosup) served as the positive control and those without any treatment served as the negative control. Briefly, 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein (DCFH-DA) was added to the bacterial suspensions containing GCDs and incubated for 30 min at 37 °C with shaking. ROS concentration was determined by measuring the fluorescence (λex = 480 nm and λem = 525 nm). The enzymatic activities of SOD and CAT were determined using kits.

Cytotoxicity assay was performed following our published method [17]. Briefly, HEpG2, PC-12, and EA. hy926 were cultured in 96-well plates (DMEM medium，37 °C, 5% CO2, approximately 1 × 105 cells per well) for 24 h. Then different concentrations of GCDs were added and incubated for 24 h. Cell activity was determined using the CCK-8 kit.

The hemolysis assay of GCDs was performed based on a previous report [44]. Red blood cells (RBCs) from fresh human blood were collected by centrifugation at 1200 rpm for 15 min and washed 3 times with saline. GCDs were diluted to different concentrations using saline. Then 50 μL of concentrated RBCs were added to 750 μL of GCDs solution, and incubated for 2 h at 37 °C. The yellowish-brown color of GCDs at high concentrations (800–1600 μg/mL) interferes with the results of hemolysis test. Therefore, the concentrations of GCDs used in this experiment were 400 μg/mL and lower. The suspension was centrifuged at 1000 rpm for 15 min. The hemolysis ratio was analyzed by measuring the OD540 values of hemoglobin in the supernatant. Saline and DI water were used as negative control and positive control, respectively. The hemolysis ratio was calculated with the formula:

All data are expressed as the mean ± standard deviation (SD). Duncan's test was used to analyze the differences between the data sets. Results with P values of <0.05 were considered statistically significant.

TEM images of the GCDs prepared by hydrothermal method are shown in Fig. 1A. GCDs had a clear graphite lattice structure with a lattice stripe spacing of 0.21 nm, same as the (102) diffractive plane of sp2 graphitic carbon [17].The particle size ranged from 1.5 to 4.5 nm with an average particle size of 3.1 ± 0.23 nm (Fig. 1B). The UV–vis absorption spectrum shows a typical absorption peak of GCDs at 280 nm (Fig. 1C), which can be attributed to the π-π* leap of CC or CO, indicating the formation of a carbon structure in the graphite core [45]. GCDs exhibited excitation-dependent fluorescence (photoluminescence, PL) from 300 to 400 nm, and the optimal excitation wavelength of GCDs was 330 nm and the optimal emission wavelength was 442 nm (Fig. 1C and D). The GCDs solution was pale yellow color under fluorescent light and produced blue fluorescence under UV excitation at 365 nm (Fig. 1C). The XRD pattern of GCDs (Fig. S1) shows a broad diffraction peak of GCDs at 22.1°, corresponding to the (002) face of graphitic carbon with a layer spacing of 0.40 nm. FTIR spectroscopy was applied to analyze the functional groups on GCDs. As shown in Fig. 1E, the broad peak centered at 3367.6 cm−1 and the narrow peak at 1516.5 cm−1 are attributed to the NH stretching and bending vibrations, respectively; the absorption peak at 1192.0 cm−1 can be attributed to the CN stretching vibration, indicating the presence of amide or -NH2 on GCDs surface [46]. The peaks at 3286.6, 1697.9, and 1020.9 cm−1 is caused by the stretching vibration of OH, CO, and CO, respectively, suggesting the presence of -COOH on the surface of GCDs [47]. The absorption peaks at 2929.6 cm−1 and 1362.8 cm−1 are caused by the stretching vibration of CH and the bending vibration of CH, respectively. The absorption peak at 768.5 cm−1 can be attributed to the CS stretching vibration. In addition, the elemental composition of GCDs and their respective atomic percentages were analyzed using XPS. The elemental contents (atomic percentages) of carbon, oxygen, nitrogen, and sulfur in GCDs were 73.62%, 16.32%, 9.62%, and 0.44%, respectively (Fig. 1F). C1s can be divided into four peaks of 288.1 eV (C=O/O-C=O), 285.9 eV (C-O/C-N/C-S), 284.7 eV (CC) and 283.1 eV (C=C) (Fig. 1G). O1s had absorption peaks at 531.6 eV and 530.0 eV, corresponding to C-OH/C-O-C and CO, respectively (Fig. 1H). N1s had five absorption peaks, including 401.3 eV (quaternary-N), 400.3 eV (pyrrolic N), 399.5 eV (R-NH2), 398.6 eV (pyridine N) and 397.7 eV (CN) (Fig. 1I). The results of FTIR and XPS analysis showed that the surface of GCDs was rich in -OH, CN, -COOH and -NH2 groups, which may come from carboxylates and amino acids present in garlic juice; GCDs surface may also contain a small amount of sulfur-containing groups (CS and C-SOx) (Fig. S2), which may come from organic sulfides such as thiosulfites in garlic juice [48].

As shown in Fig. 2 , the MICs of garlic juice for E. coli, MRSA, and MRST were 375 μg/mL, 300 μg/mL, and 400 μg/mL, respectively. MICs of GCDs for E. coli, MRSA, and MRST were 50, 75, and 25 μg/mL, respectively. The MICs of GCDs were 8.3-fold (E. coli), 12-fold (MRSA), and 5.3-fold (MRST) lower than those of garlic juice. Notably, although GCDs were slightly less effective than the traditional antibiotics kanamycin and rifampicin against E. coli, GCDs were much more effective than kanamycin and rifampicin against the MDR bacteria. Furthermore, as shown in Table 1 , the antimicrobial efficacy of chemical-derived CDs is typically superior than that of biomass-derived CDs. While the MICs of GCDs for both conventional and MDR bacteria were comparable to those of chemical-derived CDs.

Based on the MIC results, we further evaluated the bactericidal effects of garlic juice and GCDs against three bacteria. As seen in Fig. 3A, B, and C, after co-cultivation with bacteria for 240 min, garlic juice and GCDs exhibited concentration-dependent antibacterial activity against E. coli, MRSA, and MRST. The bactericidal concentrations of GCDs were significantly lower than those of their precursor substance, garlic juice. Therefore, only GCDs were used in the subsequent experiments. The bactericidal kinetics of GCDs were investigated at different concentrations (Fig. 3D, E, and F). As shown in Fig. 3D, all E. coli population was decreased by 2.84 Log CFU/mL after 240 min of incubation with 50 μg/mL GCDs. Increasing the concentrations of GCDs facilitated bacterial die-off. A decline of 4.75 Log CFU/mL was observed after 240 min treatment with 100 μg/mL GCDs; all E. coli was killed in 200 μg/mL GCDs after 150 min. A faster die-off was observed in MRSA. All bacterial cells were killed after 240, 150, and 90 min of incubation in 50, 100, and 200 μg/mL GCDs, respectively (Fig. 3E). MRST populations were decreased by 2.25, 3.43, and 6.53 Log CFU/mL after 240 min of treatment with 50, 100, and 200 μg/mL GCDs, respectively (Fig. 3F). The antimicrobial effect of GCDS against the three strains was in the following order: MRSA > E. coli > MRST. The superior antibacterial effect of GCDs against Gram-positive MRSA than Gram-negative MRST and E. coli may be attributed to the differences in the cell wall and outer cell membrane structures which can affect the interaction and internalization of CDs with bacteria [46]. The antibacterial effect of GCDs on MRSA and MRST was quite different, which aroused our interest. Therefore, MRSA and MRST were used in the following experiments.

Given that biofilm formation often leads to increased bacterial resistance to antimicrobial agents [5], the anti-biofilm activity of GCDs was also evaluated on MDR bacteria (Fig. 4A and B). The results of crystalline violet staining showed that the inhibition rate of GCDs on MRSA and MRST biofilm formation increased significantly with increasing concentrations. The inhibitory effect was superior on MRSA than that on MRST. At 1600 μg/mL, GCDs inhibited biofilm formation of MRSA and MRST by 91.78% and 66.23%, respectively. GCDs at 100 μg/mL or lower had no significant inhibitory effect against biofilm formation.

Firstly, we observed MRSA and MRST cells treated with 200 μg/mL GCDs for different times by fluorescence live and dead cell staining. SYTO 9 can label all bacteria, showing green fluorescence, while PI can only label bacterial cells with damaged cell membranes, showing red fluorescence [58]. As seen in Fig. 5A and B, only green fluorescence was observed in both control groups, indicating that the cell membranes were intact. In contrast, the green fluorescence signal of both bacteria was diminished at 60 min of treatment with GCDs, and a stronger red fluorescence signal was observed, indicating cell membrane damages of MRSA and MRST. As the treatment time increased to 120 min, only very little green fluorescence signal was observed in the SYTO 9 channel of MRSA and MRST, and the red fluorescence signal was strong in the PI channel, resulting in almost entirely red fluorescence signal in the merge images. This indicates that the cell membranes of most bacteria were severely damaged. The SEM results further confirmed the results of fluorescence staining. As shown in Fig. 5C and D, after 60 min of GCDs treatment, MRSA cells showed different degrees of shrinkage and pitting, and MRST cells also showed perforation and breakage on their surfaces (indicated by red arrows). After 120 min of treatment, the structural integrity of most MRSA and MRST cells was significantly disrupted, resulting in morphological changes and cell breakage (indicated by red arrows).

We then measured the extracellular AKP activity, protein concentration, and nucleic acids (OD260) content in the bacteria-GCDs mixture to further investigate the changes in cell wall and cell membrane permeability. AKP is a protease that exists between the cell membrane and the cell wall, and it only leaks out when the cell wall is damaged; therefore, AKP activity was used to assess the degree of damage to the cell wall [43]. The AKP activity of the GCDs-treated groups showed a time- and concentration-dependent response to GCDs. For example, in the 200 μg/mL GCDs treatment group, the AKP activity increased sharply at 60 min and stabilized at the same level after 120 min (Fig. 6A and B). The trends of protein and nucleic acid concentrations in the bacterial solutions were similar to the changes of AKP activity (Fig. 6C-F). The above results collectively show that GCDs can damage the cell wall and cell membrane of MDR bacteria.

Subsequently, we investigated the interaction between GCDs and bacterial cells. As shown in Fig. 7A, the ζ-potential of GCDs was +8.53 ± 0.41 eV, and the ζ-potentials of MRSA and MRST were − 25.44 ± 1.61 eV and − 20.16 ± 0.56 eV, respectively. The positive charges of GCDs were mainly derived from the surface-rich nitrogen-containing groups (Fig. 1I: Quaternary-N, pyrrolic N, pyridine N). The negative charge of Gram-positive bacteria comes from teichoic acid and lipoteichoic acid, and that of Gram-positive bacteria comes from phospholipids and lipopolysaccharides [59]. Electrostatic interactions between positively charged GCDs and negatively charged bacteria facilitated the adsorption of GCDs to the bacteria, disrupting the surface charge and further leading to cell membrane damage and ultimately bacterial death. Increased cell membrane permeability also leads to easier entry of GCDs into bacterial cells [60]. As shown in Fig. 7B, MRSA and MRST treated with 50 μg/mL GCDs for 45 min showed blue fluorescence, which overlapped well with the bright field channel, indicating the internalization of GCDs into bacteria [61].

It is worth noting that the cell wall of Gram-positive bacteria is a loose monolayer structure composed of peptidoglycan and phosphopeptidic acid, and GCDs can easily pass through the porous cell wall and enter the bacterial cells [33]. In contrast, the cell wall of Gram-negative bacteria is a dense bilayer structure, including a peptidoglycan layer and an outer membrane layer [62]. The outer membrane is rich in small pore protein channels and lipopolysaccharides cross-linked by divalent cations, which may weaken the binding to GCDs and impede their entry into the cell [63–65].These special structures reduce the penetration of GCDs into Gram-negative bacteria [66], resulting in a stronger antibacterial effect of GCDs against Gram-positive MRSA than Gram-negative MRST.

To investigate the mechanism of damage caused by GCDs inside bacterial cells, we tested the intracellular ROS levels after GCDs treatment using DCFH-DA as a fluorescent indicator. DCFH-DA is an ester compound that freely enters the bacterial cell and is hydrolyzed by esterase to DCFH that cannot penetrate the cell membrane [67]. ROS in bacterial cells can further oxidize DCFH to produce 2′,7-dichlorodihydrofluorescein (DCF), which has maximum fluorescence emission at 525 nm. The fluorescence intensity of DCF can directly reflect the level of ROS in bacterial cells [68]. As shown in Fig. 8A and B, intracellular ROS levels of MRSA and MRST significantly increased in a concentration-dependent manner after 30 min of GCDs treatment compared to the negative control. The ROS levels of both MRSA and MRST were higher than the positive control Rosup at a GCDs concentration of 200 μg/mL, but at a lower GCDs concentration of 50 μg/mL, the ROS levels of MRST were not significantly elevated compared to the control, while the ROS levels of MRSA remained significantly higher than the control.

We then tested the changes in the activity of SOD and CAT of the bacteria during GCDs treatment. SOD and CAT can scavenge free radicals and inhibit their reactive propagation in cells; they are key enzymes for cellular resistance to oxidative stress [43]. As seen in Fig. 8C, after treatment with 50 μg/mL GCDs, the SOD activity of MRSA first increased and then decreased. This change was progressively more dramatic as the concentration of GCDs increased. The SOD activity of MRSA was elevated to 5.21 of the initial enzyme activities after 15 min of treatment with 200 μg/mL GCDs. The SOD activity then decreased sharply, and after 60 min of treatment, the SOD activity decreased to 19.3% of the initial enzyme activity. The SOD activity of MRST showed a similar trend, but when the concentration of GCDs was low (50 and 100 μg/mL), the trend was flatter compared to MRSA. The decrease in SOD activity may originate from the inhibitory effect of GCDs [46]. The trend of CAT activity of both bacteria was similar to that of SOD activity (Fig. 8E and F), but the overall change was not as drastic as that of SOD.

The above results suggest that GCDs induced ROS production inside bacterial cells, causing oxidative stress. The increase in GCDs concentration leads to upregulation of SOD and CAT activity, which is an adaptive response of bacteria to oxidative stress [69]. With the extension of treatment time, the excessive accumulation of ROS exceeded the tolerance limit of antioxidant enzymes, at which time the enzyme activity was blunted and decreased sharply [43]. At the same time, GCDs also inhibits the activity of SOD and CAT [46], and eventually a large amount of ROS that cannot be cleared causes irreversible damage to the bacteria.

Overall, the antimicrobial mechanisms of GCDs rely mainly on disruption of bacterial cell walls and cell membranes, as well as induction of bacterial oxidative stress response. Firstly, GCDs bounded to the bacterial surface via electrostatic adsorption between the positively charged groups on its surface and the negatively charged groups on the bacterial membranes [70]. GCDs adsorbed on the surface of bacteria can change the potential difference between inside and outside of the bacterial cell membrane, which can affect the normal function of membrane ion channels, disrupt ion homeostasis, and eventually damage cell membrane [60]. GCDs also interfere with the normal physiological function of transmembrane proteins such as channels, pore proteins or receptors, thus interfering with the proton pool in the intermembrane space or electron transport along the respiratory chain [71]. Electrons with blocked transport in the respiratory chain are easily transferred to oxygen molecules to form superoxide anion radicals (O2∙ ̅), which further react with other molecules leading to a large accumulation of ROS. Excess intracellular ROS can disrupt normal cellular nucleic acid, protein and lipid metabolism, causing oxidative damage to cell membranes and destroy cell walls [67]. Moreover, damage to the cell wall and cell membrane facilitate the entry of GCDs into the bacteria, inhibiting the activity of antioxidant enzymes, further aggravating the oxidative stress of the bacteria, and eventually causing bacterial death.

The biocompatibility of GCDs was evaluated by cytotoxicity assay and hemolysis assay. GCDs at concentrations up to 1600 μg/mL had no significant impact on the survival rates of HEpG2, PC-12, and EA. hy926 (Fig. 9A, B, and C). In addition, no hemolysis was observed at GCDs concentrations of up to 400 μg/mL. This indicates that GCDs had good hemocompatibility and didn't cause rupture of RBCs under physiological conditions. The above results collectively showed that GCDs were an effective and safe antimicrobial agent.

In conclusion, we have synthesized GCDs using with inhibitory effects against MDR bacteria and their biofilms by an environmentally friendly hydrothermal method and comprehensively investigated their antibacterial mechanisms. The characterization of GCDs showed a positively charged surface with excellent fluorescence properties and a very small particle size. Antibacterial tests have demonstrated that GCDs are far more effective than conventional antibiotics (kanamycin and rifampicin) against MDR bacteria and can inhibit their biofilm formation. Studies on the antibacterial mechanism have shown that GCDs can adsorb to negatively charged bacterial surfaces and disrupt the normal function of cell membranes, causing membrane damage and excessive accumulation of ROS. At the same time, due to their extremely small particle size, some of the GCDs will enter the bacteria and inhibit the activity of antioxidant enzymes, which will intensify the oxidative stress of bacteria and cause cell death. More importantly, cytotoxicity assays and hemolysis assays demonstrated that GCDs have sound biocompatibility, which confers a broad application potential for GCDs. This work provides an important idea for the rational use of natural biomass against MDR bacterial infections.
